narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your undergraduate GPA of 2.60, LSAT score of 32, high family income bracket, and plans to attend full-time, along with your school deciles from Year 1 (3rd decile) and Year 3 (2nd decile). The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on profiles like yours, estimated a 54.6% chance that you would fail the bar exam—crossing the threshold that led to your application's rejection. This prediction stems from weighing various aspects of your background against patterns seen in thousands of past students. The most influential factor pushing toward a failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking, which placed you in the 2nd decile—well below the typical 5.5th decile average—suggesting relatively lower performance in later undergraduate years compared to peers who passed the bar. Next, your undergraduate GPA of 2.60 worked in your favor, as it pulled the prediction somewhat toward passing, being closer to what successful students often show despite being under the 3.21 average. Similarly, your high family income provided a positive nudge, countering the risk since students from similar brackets tend to fare better on the bar. While your LSAT score of 32 is below the 36.7 average and your full-time status aligns with most applicants, these played smaller roles here. Overall, your profile compares to typical admitted students by having strengths in socioeconomic support but notable gaps in later academic deciles that outweighed them for this model. It's understandable to feel disappointed, especially as a first-generation aspirant in your community pushing through these challenges. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your later-year grades through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and gathering strong recommendation letters highlighting your resilience. Many students in similar situations have improved their profiles and succeeded—reapplying after targeted preparation could shift your odds significantly.
